title: Honoring Zeta Phi Beta Sorority, Inc., on reaching the historic milestone of 105 years of scholarship, service, sisterhood, and finer womanhood.
plain_summary: This congressional resolution celebrates Zeta Phi Beta Sorority, Inc., for reaching 105 years of service. The sorority was started in 1920 by five women at Howard University and has grown to over 100,000 members around the world. The resolution honors the sorority's work in education, community service, and supporting women. It also recognizes the important achievements of many sorority members over the years.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.
subjects: Social work, volunteer service, charitable organizations

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution honors Zeta Phi Beta Sorority, Inc., for reaching one hundred and five years of scholarship, service, sisterhood, and finer womanhood.

sections.title: Founding and Founders
sections.text: Zeta Phi Beta Sorority, Inc., was started on January sixteenth, nineteen twenty, at Howard University in Washington, DC. Five women founded it: Arizona Cleaver Stemons, Pearl Anna Neal, Myrtle Tyler Faithful, Viola Tyler Goings, and Fannie Pettie Watts. These five founders were called the Five Pearls. They wanted to provide true service, support education, and set a standard for sisterly love and being a fine woman. On March thirtieth, nineteen twenty-three, the sorority became legally incorporated in Washington, DC, to make sure the organization would last forever. Five women made this happen: Myrtle Tyler Faithful, Gladys Warrington, Joanna Houston, Josephine F. Johnson, and L.O. Goldia Smith.

sections.title: Growth and Achievements
sections.text: Since nineteen twenty, Zeta Phi Beta Sorority, Inc., has created hundreds of local chapters around the world. It now has more than one hundred thousand members. The sorority is divided into eight regions that span continents and has over eight hundred and seventy-five chapters in the United States, Africa, Europe, Asia, and the Caribbean. The organization has achieved many firsts. It was the first National Pan-Hellenic Council organization (a group of historically Black Greek organizations) to have a central headquarters. It was the first to start a chapter in Africa, the first to form helper groups, and the first to be officially connected to a fraternity called Phi Beta Sigma Fraternity, Inc. The sorority works hard to start chapters at both predominantly white schools and historically Black colleges and universities.

sections.title: Mission and Notable Members
sections.text: Zeta Phi Beta Sorority, Inc., stays true to its mission statement. The organization aims to help people and communities by raising awareness, encouraging the highest standards for school achievement, and creating greater unity among members. Many famous women have been members of this sorority. These include Bernette Johnson, who was the first Black woman to serve on the Louisiana Supreme Court and the first Black woman to become Chief Justice. Syleena Johnson is a Grammy Award nominee R&B singer. Dr. Elizabeth Koontz was a well-known educator. Dionne Warwick was a former Goodwill Ambassador to the United Nations Food and Agriculture Organization. Zora Neale Hurston was an author, filmmaker, and anthropologist. Sheryl Underwood is a comedian and television host.

sections.title: Congressional Resolution
sections.text: Zeta Phi Beta Sorority, Inc., continues to make a difference in the United States and around the world. The sorority always follows in the footsteps of its founders and lives by its motto to be a community-conscious, action-oriented organization. Therefore, the House of Representatives congratulates and commends Zeta Phi Beta Sorority, Inc., on one hundred and five years of scholarship, service, sisterhood, and finer womanhood.
